issues: JWT_SECRET hardcoded as a string literal in source — must be loaded from environment variable
No rate limiting on /login — trivially vulnerable to brute-force and credential stuffing attacks
No payload size limit on express.json() — large JSON bodies can cause DoS
bcrypt truncates passwords silently at 72 bytes — passwords longer than 72 chars all produce the same hash, a known security pitfall
No email format validation — arbitrary strings accepted as email
No HTTPS enforcement or Secure/HttpOnly cookie handling (token exposed in JSON body)
No password minimum strength enforcement
req.body fields accessed without checking if req.body itself is defined — malformed Content-Type can yield undefined body
Global error handler present but only catches synchronous middleware errors, not async route errors that are already handled internally
No logging of error details server-side (err swallowed silently in catch blocks) — makes debugging impossible in production
JWT_SECRET should be read from process.env, not hardcoded
User ID assigned as users.length + 1 — not unique after deletions, race-condition-prone in concurrent scenarios
No email normalization (e.g., lowercase trim) — 'User@example.com' and 'user@example.com' treated as different users
No separation of concerns — routes, middleware, data store, and server bootstrap all in a single file
No input sanitization beyond presence check — whitespace-only strings pass validation
No rate limiting (express-rate-limit or similar) — login endpoint open to brute force
No express.json() size limit — default 100kb but not explicitly set; large payloads risk memory pressure
No email format validation with regex — any non-empty string accepted
No password maximum length enforcement — bcrypt 72-byte truncation silently degrades security
In-memory users array grows unbounded with no eviction or size cap
User ID strategy (users.length + 1) breaks after any deletion — not production-safe
No request timeout configured — slow clients can hold connections indefinitely
